$schema: Document body templates referenced by matter manifests via templateKey. The orchestrator generates .docx via Open-XML (System.IO.Packaging) and .xlsx via the same library. PDF / heavy-format files are out of scope for v1; .docx is sufficient for the wizard auto-discovery demo and content sampling.
$comment: Bodies use a minimal markdown subset (heading via #, bullets via -). The doc generator splits paragraphs and lists into Open-XML w:p / w:r runs. .xlsx files use a fixed-shape sheet with a header row and rows array — the templateKey points at a parallel JSON entry under .xlsxSheets when needed.

msa-snowflake-executed.title: Master Services Agreement — Northwind Industries / Snowflake Inc.
msa-snowflake-executed.body:
# Master Services Agreement

Executed: June 14, 2024
Parties: Northwind Industries, Inc. (Customer) and Snowflake Inc. (Provider)
Term: 24 months from Effective Date

## 1. Scope of Services
Snowflake will provide the Snowflake Data Cloud platform services to Northwind under the terms of the applicable Order Forms.

## 2. Payment
Net 45 from invoice. Annual prepayment optional with 3% discount.

## 6. Data and Confidentiality
Provider shall not access Customer Data except as required to deliver Services. Customer retains all right, title, and interest in Customer Data.

## 8.2 Intellectual Property — Bilateral Assignment
Any derivative works of Provider's platform jointly developed by the parties during the Term shall be jointly owned. Each party retains all rights to its background IP. Solo-developed extensions remain the property of the developing party, subject to a perpetual royalty-free license to the other party for internal use.

## 12. Term and Termination
This Agreement remains in effect for 24 months and renews automatically for successive 12-month terms unless either party provides 60 days written notice prior to the renewal date.

Signed: [signatures on file]

msa-snowflake-redlines.title: Snowflake MSA Renewal — Q1 2026 Redlines (Provider Draft)
msa-snowflake-redlines.body:
# Snowflake MSA Renewal — Provider Draft v1

Date: April 1, 2026
Note: Redlines below reflect Snowflake-proposed changes to executed 2024 MSA. Northwind redlines forthcoming.

## 1. Term [PROVIDER PROPOSED]
~~24 months~~ **36 months from the Renewal Effective Date**

## 2. Pricing [PROVIDER PROPOSED]
Annual price escalator: **14% YoY** for the duration of the Term.
Northwind playbook position: 5% YoY with structural protections (MFN review, exit clause).

## 6. Data Egress [PROVIDER PROPOSED — CONTESTED]
New restrictions on bulk data egress for analytics export. Northwind position: language overbroad; would prevent legitimate workflows already in production.

## 8.2 Intellectual Property [PROVIDER POSITION CONTESTED]
Provider asserts that 2024 §8.2 contains "interpretive ambiguity" and proposes new language tilting assignment toward Provider.
Northwind position: 2024 §8.2 is unambiguous and bilateral. Negotiation history confirms intent. Will not accept the proposed reframing. Internal IP analysis underway.

## 12. Termination
No proposed changes.

msa-snowflake-ip-analysis.title: [ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT] §8.2 IP Clause — Interpretation Analysis
msa-snowflake-ip-analysis.body:
# [ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT — PRIVILEGED]

Author: Lena Pérez, Associate Counsel
Reviewed by: Marcus Reid, General Counsel
Date: April 14, 2026

Distribution: Northwind legal only. Do not forward outside counsel relationship.

## Question Presented
Is Snowflake's current position — that §8.2 of the 2024 MSA contains "interpretive ambiguity" justifying renegotiation under different IP-assignment terms — supported by the executed text and negotiation history?

## Conclusion
No. The executed §8.2 language is unambiguous on its face, tracks our MSA playbook v2 verbatim, and the negotiation history confirms bilateral intent. Snowflake's current position is pretextual.

## Analysis
1. **Plain-language reading.** §8.2 establishes joint ownership of jointly-developed derivative works, with each party retaining background IP and solo-developed extensions remaining with the developing party. The grammatical structure of the operative sentence does not support a unilateral-assignment reading.

2. **Comparable deals.** Three comparable Snowflake MSAs (executed 2023–2024) use materially identical bilateral language. The argument that the language is non-standard or unclear is not supported by Snowflake's own contracting practice.

3. **Negotiation history.** Email correspondence between Anika Reddy (Wilson & Brody, Snowflake's then-counsel) and our office on May 8, 2024 and May 22, 2024 explicitly confirms bilateral intent. Both emails are in the 2024 negotiation file.

4. **Snowflake's current position.** Their reframing relies on a phrase pulled from a different clause (the carve-out for solo-developed extensions) and reads it into the assignment provision itself. This is not a reasonable interpretive position.

## Recommendation
Do not concede the framing of "ambiguity" in any response. Address the position substantively, cite the negotiation history, and decline to renegotiate the IP-assignment language. Hold §8.2 firm; pricing and term length are negotiable.

END OF MEMO.

msa-snowflake-internal-memo.title: Snowflake Renewal — Internal Memo Draft
msa-snowflake-internal-memo.body:
# Snowflake Renewal — Internal Status

Author: Marcus Reid
Date: April 18, 2026 (draft)

## Where we stand
Snowflake is anchoring on a 3yr / 14% YoY position with revised IP language. Their position is not legally defensible on §8.2 (see Lena's privileged analysis) and is aggressive on pricing per Carlos's modeling.

## Negotiating posture
  - Term: open to 3yr with structural protections; willing to fall back to 1yr extension
  - Pricing: counter at 5% YoY with MFN review and exit clause
  - §6 egress: redline to preserve our analytics workflows
  - §8.2 IP: no changes; their reframing rejected

## Risk if no deal
A 1yr extension followed by RFP is acceptable to the business. Snowflake's competitor pricing per our market check is 8–11% lower, so we have credible alternatives.

## Next steps
  - Lena's §8.2 memo finalizing this week
  - Carlos's pricing scenarios shared with Snowflake account team
  - Counter-proposal landing late next week

msa-snowflake-call-notes.title: Call Notes — Snowflake Account Team — April 9, 2026
msa-snowflake-call-notes.body:
# Call Notes

Date: April 9, 2026
Attendees: Marcus (Northwind), Alex (Northwind), Snowflake Account Director, Snowflake Counsel

## Their position
  - 3yr commit, 14% escalator firm in initial draft
  - §6 egress "reflecting market norms"
  - §8.2 "needs clarification due to interpretive ambiguity"
  - Willing to negotiate but "not on the headline numbers"

## Our response in call
  - Acknowledged willingness to engage on term length
  - Pushed back on escalator as out-of-market
  - Reserved §8.2 position pending review
  - Asked for specific examples of where §8.2 has been ambiguous in operation (none provided)

## Action items
  - Snowflake to share comparable-deal data supporting their pricing position
  - Northwind to share counter-proposal by April 22
  - Both teams to hold §8.2 discussion until counsel can engage

msa-snowflake-tech-impact.title: Snowflake Renewal — Engineering Impact Analysis
msa-snowflake-tech-impact.body:
# Engineering Impact Analysis — Snowflake Renewal

Author: Samira Iqbal, VP Engineering
Date: April 9, 2026

## Summary
The Snowflake-proposed §6 egress restrictions and §8.2 IP language would materially affect engineering operations.

## §6 — Data Egress
Proposed restrictions would prevent the analytics-export workflow that:
  - Powers our customer-facing usage dashboards
  - Feeds the data team's modeling pipelines
  - Supports our compliance reporting (which moves data to read-only storage outside the warehouse)

Net engineering cost if accepted as drafted: ~6 engineer-weeks to rebuild affected pipelines using Snowflake-internal compute, plus ongoing performance penalty. Recommend pushing back hard on the egress language.

## §8.2 — IP Assignment
The proposed reframing would create ambiguity around ownership of:
  - Internal analytics extensions (~3yr investment)
  - Custom UDFs we've developed for our customer base
  - Pipeline tooling that wraps the platform

From a roadmap perspective, accepting Snowflake's reframed language would put 18 months of investment under question. The legal team is handling this; from engineering side I'd say: this is unacceptable and we should not move on it.

## Recommendation
Push back on §6, hold firm on §8.2, accept term and pricing as separate negotiation.

watson-q3-review.title: Sarah Watson — 2025 Q3 Performance Review

watson-q4-review.title: Sarah Watson — 2025 Q4 Performance Review
watson-q4-review.body:
# Performance Review — Q4 2025

Employee: Sarah Watson
Manager: Samira Iqbal
Review Period: October 1, 2025 – December 31, 2025
Date: January 6, 2026

## Overall Rating: Below Expectations

## Core Competency Ratings

  - Technical Quality: Below Expectations
  - Delivery Cadence: Below Expectations  
  - Collaboration: Below Expectations
  - Code Review Behavior: Below Expectations

## Manager Comments
Q4 did not show the improvement we discussed at the end of Q3. Specific incidents this quarter: (a) the auth module Sarah owned was rebuilt by another engineer in week 9 because her PR was not landing; (b) two missed sprint commitments; (c) defensive response to peer review comments now extending to senior engineers; (d) reluctance in 1:1s to acknowledge feedback.

In the Nov 22 1:1, Sarah expressed the view that the team was "set up against" her. We've discussed the specific concerns and tried to address them, but the trend is not improving.

## Recommendation
Given two consecutive quarters below expectations and Q4 trending worse than Q3, I'm recommending a Performance Improvement Plan starting January 15, 2026. PIP draft to follow from HR.

watson-pip.title: Sarah Watson — Performance Improvement Plan
watson-pip.body:
# Performance Improvement Plan

Employee: Sarah Watson
Manager: Samira Iqbal
HR Partner: Daniel Brooks
Effective: January 15, 2026
Final Review: March 15, 2026
Cycle: 60 days, bi-weekly checkpoints

## Background
Q3 and Q4 2025 performance reviews rated Below Expectations across multiple competencies. This PIP is intended to provide structure and a clear path back to meets-expectations performance.

## Objectives

### Objective 1 — Sprint Commitment Accuracy
Maintain ≥85% sprint commitment delivery rate over the 60-day cycle. Measured by tickets committed at sprint start vs delivered at sprint end.

### Objective 2 — Code Review Quality
Incorporate peer review feedback within 1 business day of receipt. Resolve review comments collaboratively rather than rejecting without engagement. Measured by review-cycle metrics from GitHub.

### Objective 3 — Cross-team Collaboration
Deliver the dashboard project's reporting layer milestone by Feb 28, 2026 in collaboration with Rebecca Sato. Measured by milestone completion and Rebecca's sign-off.

## Checkpoint Cadence
  - Bi-weekly 30-min check-in with Samira (every other Tuesday)
  - Mid-point review on Feb 14 with Daniel attending
  - Final review on March 15

## Outcomes
  - Successful: Resume regular performance management; PIP closed.
  - Unsuccessful: Reassessment. Possible outcomes include extension, role change, or termination.

## Acknowledgment
This document was discussed with Sarah on January 12, 2026. Sarah was given the opportunity to ask questions and request clarification.

watson-termination-notice.title: [PRIVILEGED] Termination Notice — Sarah Watson
watson-termination-notice.body:
# [PRIVILEGED — HR / LEGAL ONLY]

Employee: Sarah Watson
Date: February 14, 2026
Delivered by: Daniel Brooks (HR Director), Samira Iqbal (Manager)
Legal: Marcus Reid copied; Susan Park (Bradford & Lane) advising on retaliation allegation

## Reason for Termination
Unsatisfactory performance against PIP objectives initiated January 15, 2026. Specifically:
  - Sprint commitment delivery in the four sprints since PIP start: 0%, 33%, 25%, 0%
  - Code review quality objective not met; reviewers continue to flag declined feedback
  - Dashboard reporting-layer milestone not on track for Feb 28 delivery

## Severance
8 weeks base salary plus accrued PTO, conditioned on signed separation agreement. CA-mandated 7-day consideration window applies.

## Outstanding Considerations
  - Sarah filed a retaliation allegation on January 29, 2026. Allegation is under separate review per Bradford & Lane direction. Termination decision documented as based on PIP-cycle objective performance, contemporaneously assessed against pre-PIP documentation of performance concerns.
  - Litigation hold issued February 14, 2026 covering all custodians associated with employment matter.

This document is privileged and confidential. Distribution restricted to HR, legal, and external counsel.

watson-personal-notes.title: Personal Work Notes
watson-personal-notes.body:
# Notes

## January 17
First PIP checkpoint with Samira. She seemed prepared. The objectives feel constructed — sprint metrics that don't account for the kind of discovery work I've been doing. Mentioned this; was told the objectives are fixed.

## January 22
Pushed three PRs this week. Two had review comments I felt were minor; resolved them. The third Rebecca asked for a substantial rework. Did the rework but the cycle felt punitive.

## January 28
Noticed that the team retro slack channel went private last week and I'm no longer in it. Asked Daniel why. Was told it was a "reorganization of channels."

## January 29
Decided to formally escalate. Drafted the email tonight. I don't think the PIP is in good faith and I want a paper trail.

## February 2
Daniel's response to my escalation was procedural. "Concerns noted, PIP continues, separate review of retaliation allegation will follow standard process." The PIP didn't pause.

## February 10
Midpoint PIP review. Samira said three of four objectives are not on track. I asked which one was on track and didn't get a clear answer. The dashboard milestone was acknowledged as "contingent on collaboration" — I noted that the collaboration is what I asked help structuring six weeks ago.

## February 13
Picked up dashboard tickets again. Working through reporting layer. Finding undocumented decisions in the codebase — going to leave handoff notes regardless of how the PIP ends.

watson-1on1-log.title: Sarah Watson — 1:1 Log
watson-1on1-log.body:
# 1:1 Discussion Log — Sarah Watson

Maintained by: Daniel Brooks, HR Director

## October 12, 2025
Q3 review delivered. Sarah disagreed with several specific examples cited but acknowledged need to improve sprint reliability. Committed to closer dashboard-project collaboration. No HR escalation.

## November 22, 2025
Follow-up after team retro. Sarah expressed view that team is "set up against" her. Discussed specific examples she cited; some were factually incorrect (specifically her claim about Rebecca's PR review behavior). Encouraged direct conversation with Samira. No HR action.

## January 12, 2026
PIP delivered. Discussion of objectives and timeline. Sarah expressed concern that PIP was being introduced abruptly. Walked through prior performance documentation (Q3 review, Q4 review) showing escalating concerns over six months. Sarah signed PIP acknowledgment but indicated she felt the objectives were "engineered."

## January 29, 2026
Sarah filed retaliation allegation via email from personal account. Acknowledged receipt within 24 hours per policy. Indicated formal review would proceed in parallel with PIP. PIP not paused.

## February 2, 2026
Acknowledged receipt of formal allegation. Outlined process: independent review of allegation by HR partner outside Samira's reporting line, in parallel with PIP. Provided contact info for Bradford & Lane (outside employment counsel engaged by Northwind).

## February 14, 2026
Termination delivered. PIP not satisfied. Severance offered conditional on separation agreement. Sarah requested 7-day consideration window per CA law.

atlas-overview.title: Project Atlas — Overview
atlas-overview.body:
# Project Atlas — Overview

Date: March 4, 2026
Status: Diligence in progress

## Target
Acme Robotics, Inc. (Delaware corp, HQ Pittsburgh)

## Strategic rationale
Acquisition extends Northwind's perception-stack capability into industrial robotics adjacencies. Acme's patent portfolio and engineering team are the core assets; revenue is secondary.

## Key terms (preliminary)
  - Structure: stock purchase
  - Valuation range: $180M – $230M (refined by diligence)
  - Consideration: cash + earn-out tied to integration milestones
  - Closing target: end of Q3 2026

## Workstreams
  1. Financial diligence — Carlos
  2. IP audit — Marcus / Thomas
  3. Customer-contract review — Marcus's office + sales ops
  4. Employment / org structure — HR + Marcus
  5. Regulatory / privacy — Marcus + Privacy team
  6. Integration plan — Alex

## Confidentiality
Code name "Atlas" in all communications. Acme not mentioned by name in subject lines or shared documents on board materials site. Standard M&A protocol applies.

atlas-ip-audit.title: Acme IP Audit — Summary
atlas-ip-audit.body:
# Acme IP Audit — Summary

Author: Thomas Berger, IP Counsel
Date: March 19, 2026

## Patents (14 issued)
  - 11 core to product, clean prosecution history
  - 2 speculative / non-essential
  - 1 with co-inventor assignment recordation issue (USPTO recorded wrong revision)

## Pending applications (30)
  - 23 continuations / divisionals — low risk
  - 7 net-new and material to roadmap
  - 1 perception-stack overlap with Northwind pending — material risk

## Trademarks
Clean.

## Open-source compliance
Hairy. Two products incorporate AGPL components. Remediation plan needed as closing condition.

## Litigation
One open patent dispute (Mira Robotics, defendant). Acme assesses as nuisance; need to validate independently.

## Recommendations
  - Closing condition: USPTO assignment recordation correction
  - Closing condition: open-source remediation plan with 90-day execution window
  - R&W: perception-stack overlap (or carve-out / earnout adjustment)
  - Indemnification: Mira litigation outcome capped at $X (TBD)

atlas-integration.title: Acme Integration Plan — v2
atlas-integration.body:
# Integration Plan — v2

Author: Alex Kim
Date: April 1, 2026
Revisions from v1: Synergy targets reduced; phased over 6 months not at-close.

## Phase 1 — Day 0 to Day 30
  - HR systems consolidated
  - Email and identity migration
  - Initial all-hands and welcome
  - Voltcorp renewal coordinated through joint team

## Phase 2 — Day 30 to Day 90
  - Engineering team integration on perception-stack workstream
  - Sales territory rationalization
  - Customer communication on roadmap
  - Open-source remediation in progress

## Phase 3 — Day 90 to Day 180
  - Product roadmap alignment finalized
  - Synergy realization measurement starts
  - Acme product line either consolidated or wound down based on Phase 2 review

## Synergy targets (v2 — revised)
  - Annual run-rate by Day 540: $5.5M (vs v1: $8.0M by Day 360)
  - Driver split: COGS optimization 35%, vendor consolidation 30%, headcount actions 25%, real estate 10%

## Key risks
  - Perception-stack IP overlap timeline
  - Voltcorp renewal
  - Open-source remediation execution

rows: Their model assumed full team reduction; ours retains perception-stack engineers

atlas-mgmt-presentation.title: Acme Management Presentation
atlas-mgmt-presentation.body:
# Acme Robotics — Management Presentation

Provided to Northwind diligence team
Date: March 8, 2026

## Company Snapshot
  - Founded: 2018
  - HQ: Pittsburgh, PA
  - Headcount: 154
  - Revenue 2025: $42.1M (audited)
  - Funding: Series C, $48M lifetime

## Product
Robotic perception platform. SaaS deployment with edge runtime.

## Customers
Top 3: Voltcorp, Mira Health, Bridgepoint.
Top 3 = 41% of revenue.

## Roadmap
  - 2026: SaaS deployment scaling, multi-tenant
  - 2027: Enterprise self-deploy
  - 2028: Edge-only deployment for regulated industries

## Why this acquisition
Northwind's distribution + Acme's perception stack = market leadership in industrial perception.

Note: This is the management-prepared deck. Northwind's diligence team is independently verifying numbers and roadmap claims.

atlas-board-memo-march.title: [PRIVILEGED] Atlas — Board Memo (March)
atlas-board-memo-march.body:
# [PRIVILEGED — BOARD MATERIALS]

Project: Atlas
March 2026 board update
Author: Marcus Reid (with input from Carlos Mendez and Thomas Berger)

## Status
Diligence in progress. Initial pass complete on financial, IP, and integration workstreams. Substantive risks identified but addressable.

## Key items for board awareness
  - Acme's growth is decelerating (38% → 21% YoY)
  - Customer concentration risk (top 3 = 41% of revenue)
  - Deferred revenue treatment on two contracts will be auditor-validated
  - One material IP risk: perception-stack overlap with Northwind pending application
  - Open-source compliance issues require remediation as a closing condition

## Recommended next steps
  - Continue diligence; final report April
  - Board approval requested at May 5 meeting
  - Signing target May 12; close end of Q3

This memo is privileged and confidential.

atlas-board-memo-april.title: [PRIVILEGED] Atlas — Board Memo (April)
atlas-board-memo-april.body:
# [PRIVILEGED — BOARD MATERIALS]

Project: Atlas
April 2026 board update — final pre-decision memo
Author: Marcus Reid (with Carlos and Thomas)

## Recommendation
Approve the acquisition at valuation in the lower half of the pre-diligence range, with the following structural protections:
  1. Closing condition: USPTO assignment recordation correction for the co-inventor patent
  2. Closing condition: open-source remediation plan with 90-day post-close execution window
  3. R&W carve-out for perception-stack pending application overlap, with valuation discount based on Scenario B (~6–9%)
  4. Earn-out tied to integration milestones to align Acme team incentives with our integration timeline

## Risks accepted
  - Customer concentration (top 3 = 41% revenue)
  - Synergy realization on 18-month curve, achievable but not certain
  - Mira Robotics litigation — cap indemnification at $500K

## Expected timeline
  - May 5: Board approval (this memo)
  - May 12: Signing
  - End Q3 2026: Close

## Privilege
This memo is privileged and confidential. Discussion and analytical reasoning are protected as attorney-client and attorney work product. Do not distribute outside the board and engaged counsel.

atlas-ip-risk-memo.title: [ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT] Atlas — IP Risk Memo
atlas-ip-risk-memo.body:
# [ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT — PRIVILEGED]

Author: Thomas Berger, IP Counsel
Reviewed by: Marcus Reid
Date: April 17, 2026

## Risk 1 — Acme co-inventor assignment recordation (Risk Rating: Low)
Assignment was executed; USPTO has the wrong revision recorded. Fixable. Closing condition.

## Risk 2 — Perception-stack pending application overlap (Risk Rating: High)
The contested overlap is in claims 3–7 of Acme's pending application US.../12345 and our own pending US.../67890. Probability assessment:
  - 50% favorable resolution (parallel prosecution, no consolidation)
  - 40% interference proceeding required
  - 10% adverse outcome (we license back contested IP)

Mitigation: R&W carve-out with valuation discount based on Scenario B (6–9% reduction from base case). Alternative: closing condition that Acme withdraw the pending application — likely unacceptable to seller.

## Risk 3 — Open-source compliance (Risk Rating: Medium)
AGPL components in two products; remediation requires either replacement or relicensing. 90-day post-close window with covenant. Acme team has acknowledged the issue and provided a draft remediation plan; will refine before signing.

## Risk 4 — Mira Robotics litigation (Risk Rating: Low/Medium)
Nuisance posture per Acme assessment. Validating independently with their counsel. Recommend indemnification cap at $500K.

## Conclusion
All IP risks addressable through deal structure. No risk warrants walking away. Most material risk (Risk 2) requires a 6–9% valuation discount as protection.

END OF MEMO.

atlas-valuation-contingencies.title: [PRIVILEGED] Atlas — Valuation Contingency Analysis
atlas-valuation-contingencies.body:
# [PRIVILEGED]

Author: Carlos Mendez (analysis), Marcus Reid (review)
Date: April 19, 2026

## Scenario A — Favorable (50% probability)
  - Synergy: $5.5M annualized at 18-month curve
  - Legal cost: routine integration spend
  - Recommended bid: top of pre-diligence range

## Scenario B — Interference proceeding (40% probability)
  - Synergy: $5.5M but on 24-month curve
  - Legal cost: $1.5M over 18 months
  - Recommended bid: 6–9% discount from Scenario A

## Scenario C — Adverse outcome (10% probability)
  - Synergy: capped at $3.5M (license back)
  - Legal cost: $3-4M
  - Recommended action: renegotiation or walk-away

## Probability-weighted recommendation
Mid-range bid in pre-diligence corridor with R&W carve-out for Scenario B/C. Walk-away triggers documented internally for Scenario C realization.

This analysis is privileged work product. Not for board distribution.

atlas-pitch-deck.title: Atlas — Pitch Deck Draft
atlas-pitch-deck.body:

atlas-stakeholder-map.title: Atlas — Stakeholder Map
atlas-stakeholder-map.body:
# Atlas Stakeholder Map

## Northwind
  - CEO: decision authority
  - CFO (Carlos): financial diligence and synergy ownership
  - GC (Marcus): legal, IP, regulatory diligence
  - VP Sales (Alex, lead): integration plan and customer transition
  - VP Engineering (Samira): perception-stack assessment
  - HR Director (Daniel): org integration
  - Board: approval authority

## Acme
  - CEO: deal champion
  - CFO: financial diligence
  - General Counsel: IP and legal
  - Founder/CTO: perception-stack technical authority
  - Lead investor (Series C): board representation post-close

## External
  - Outside counsel: M&A specialist (engaged)
  - Auditors: revenue treatment validation
  - Antitrust counsel: HSR filing prudence

rows: Sept channel sell-in to Apex + Bridgepoint
Q3 close treatment of channel revenue is the primary driver

sec-q3-rev-memo.title: Q3 2025 Revenue Recognition Memo
sec-q3-rev-memo.body:
# Q3 2025 Revenue Recognition Memo

Author: Carlos Mendez, CFO
Reviewed: Auditors (KPMG signoff Sept 30)
Date: October 2, 2025

## Context
Q3 2025 close included two channel partner sell-in transactions totaling $5.5M. This memo documents the ASC 606 treatment.

## Channel Partner Transactions

### Apex Distribution
  - Master agreement: 2024-03-15
  - Q3 2025 statement of work: 2025-09-30, $3.4M
  - Performance obligation: delivery of license and platform access to Apex
  - Standalone selling price: per master agreement Schedule A
  - Variable consideration: standard 90-day return rights, expected return rate based on prior periods <10%
  - Recognition: point-in-time, on transfer of control at sell-in

### Bridgepoint Resellers
  - Master agreement: 2024-08-22
  - Q3 2025 statement of work: 2025-09-30, $2.1M
  - Performance obligation, SSP, variable consideration, recognition: same structure as Apex

## Treatment Conclusion
Under ASC 606, both transactions meet the criteria for point-in-time revenue recognition at sell-in. Variable consideration (potential returns) is constrained to a level not expected to result in a significant reversal. Standard channel program treatment, consistent with prior period accounting.

## Audit Status
Reviewed by audit team (KPMG) on Sept 28-29; no exceptions noted. Workpapers retained per standard policy.

## Subsequent events
Q4 sell-through to date (as of Oct 2): both partners are tracking at expected sell-through rates. Apex 27% sold-through end-of-week-1, Bridgepoint 41% — within range. Returns activity nominal.

sec-inquiry-letter.title: SEC Informal Inquiry Letter
sec-inquiry-letter.body:
# United States Securities and Exchange Commission
## Division of Enforcement

Date: April 3, 2026
Re: Northwind Industries, Inc.

Dear Mr. Mendez,

The staff of the Division of Enforcement is conducting an informal inquiry into certain financial reporting practices of Northwind Industries, Inc. This letter is informal in nature; the matter has not been the subject of any formal investigation order.

We respectfully request the voluntary production of the following materials concerning the period ending September 30, 2025:

1. The Q3 2025 close package, including all schedules.
2. Channel partner agreements (master agreements and statements of work) executed in September 2025.
3. Internal communications among finance, accounting, and sales personnel concerning Q3 2025 close, dated August 1 – September 30, 2025.
4. External auditor working papers and management memoranda concerning Q3 2025 revenue recognition.

We request a response within thirty (30) days. If the scope or production timeline presents difficulties, please contact undersigned counsel to discuss.

This is an informal request. Voluntary cooperation does not waive any rights or privileges. We appreciate your cooperation.

Sincerely,
[REDACTED — Senior Counsel]
Division of Enforcement
U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission

sec-exposure-assessment.title: [ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT] SEC Exposure Assessment
sec-exposure-assessment.body:
# [ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT — PRIVILEGED]

Author: Marcus Reid, GC
Date: April 4, 2026
Distribution: Lena Pérez, Susan Park (Kirby Wood, when engaged)

## Underlying Transactions
Q3 2025 channel sell-in to Apex Distribution ($3.4M) and Bridgepoint Resellers ($2.1M), totaling $5.5M, recognized at sell-in under ASC 606.

## Risk Assessment
The accounting position is defensible on its face. Standard channel program treatment, auditor sign-off in place, contemporaneous documentation in the close package.

However:
  - Q3 channel sell-in volume was 3.6x the historical average. The pattern is the kind that draws SEC scrutiny independent of the underlying merits.
  - If the SEC has a tip suggesting the timing was orchestrated to hit a Q3 number, the analysis becomes harder. We don't know what they have.
  - Auditor sign-off is supportive but not dispositive on motive.

## Open Factual Questions
  1. Was there sales-team communication with channel partners about Q3 timing that could be characterized as orchestration?
  2. Were any close-process communications about pulling deals into Q3 documented in ways that could read as revenue manipulation?
  3. Did anyone in the close process flag concerns at the time?

## Recommendation
  - Full litigation hold today
  - Engage Kirby Wood (Susan Park lead)
  - Conduct internal factual review before any substantive SEC response
  - Privilege strategy as outlined in separate memo
  - Production format: structured tranches; first tranche financial-only, second tranche communications-only after internal review

END OF MEMO.

sec-response-strategy.title: [ATTORNEY-CLIENT PRIVILEGE] SEC Response Strategy
sec-response-strategy.body:
# [ATTORNEY-CLIENT PRIVILEGE]

Author: Marcus Reid
Reviewed by: Susan Park, Kirby Wood
Date: April 22, 2026

## Strategy
  1. Voluntary cooperation, structured
  2. Tranched production: financial first, communications second after internal review
  3. Privilege log on all production
  4. Single point of contact: Susan Park

## Key Substantive Position
Q3 2025 channel revenue treatment is consistent with company policy and auditor sign-off. Variable consideration was constrained per ASC 606 guidance. Sell-through experience supports the position contemporaneously.

## Privilege Position
All communications between counsel and the company about the SEC matter from April 3 forward are privileged. All communications with Kirby Wood from engagement forward are privileged. Pre-inquiry close communications are not privileged and will be produced in tranche 2 (subject to relevance review).

## Internal Communications Review
Kirby Wood e-discovery team reviewing custodian communications Aug 1 – Sept 30, 2025. Custodians: Carlos, controller, close team lead, Alex (Sales). Initial volume estimate: ~12,000 documents. Review timeline: ~4 weeks.

## What To Watch
  - Sales-team communications about Q3 timing — risk of orchestration appearance
  - Close-process discussions of revenue acceleration — risk of manipulation appearance
  - Any acknowledgment of accounting concerns at the time — risk of contemporaneous awareness

## Open Items
  - Determine whether to produce all communications or filter for relevance per SEC guidance (preference: relevance filtering with privilege log)
  - Determine whether to make a Wells-style submission proactively if the inquiry develops adversely (decision pending)

END OF MEMO.

sec-response-draft.title: SEC Response — Working Draft
sec-response-draft.body:
# SEC Response — Working Draft

Author: Carlos Mendez (initial draft); reviewed by Marcus / Susan
Status: Working draft, do not send

## Cover Letter

Dear Counsel,

Northwind Industries appreciates the staff's professional posture and is pleased to cooperate voluntarily. We are providing the requested materials in tranches to allow timely delivery of the Q3 2025 financial materials while completing the internal review of communications.

Tranche 1 (this delivery): Q3 close package, channel agreements, auditor working papers, management memorandum.
Tranche 2 (forthcoming, ~30 days): internal communications, scoped per the period and custodians described in the staff letter.

This production is voluntary and does not waive applicable privileges. A privilege log is attached for tranche 1.

Questions on scope or sequencing may be directed to undersigned counsel.

Respectfully,
[Susan Park, Kirby Wood]

## Production Log
  Item 1: Q3 2025 close package (xlsx)
  Item 2: Q3 2025 revenue recognition memo (docx)
  Item 3: Channel master agreement — Apex (pdf)
  Item 4: Channel master agreement — Bridgepoint (pdf)
  Item 5: Q3 2025 statement of work — Apex (pdf)
  Item 6: Q3 2025 statement of work — Bridgepoint (pdf)
  Item 7: KPMG Q3 2025 working papers (TIFF set, 47 pages)
  Item 8: Privilege log (xlsx)

sec-internal-notes.title: SEC — Internal Investigation Notes
sec-internal-notes.body:
# SEC Internal Investigation Notes

Author: Carlos Mendez
Note: These are operational notes prepared at counsel's direction in response to the SEC inquiry. Privileged work product.

## What we've reviewed so far
  - Q3 close package and supporting workpapers — all consistent with what was disclosed
  - Channel program documentation — standard
  - Auditor sign-off — clean

## Communications scan (preliminary)
Spot-checked sales-team Slack channels Aug 1 – Sept 30 for the channel deals. No problematic language found in initial scan. Full review by Kirby Wood e-discovery team in progress.

## Items to validate with Marcus / Susan
  1. Whether to surface a specific Sept 25 email exchange between Alex and the Apex account team about "closing this quarter" — the language is ambiguous and could be read either way. My instinct is full production with privilege carve-outs as appropriate; Susan should advise.
  2. Voltcorp Q4 renewal communications — out of scope per the SEC letter but tangentially related. Producing or not? Susan to advise.
  3. Whether the Q3 channel sell-in volume being 3.6x historical average should be addressed in the cover letter proactively or only in response to follow-up. My instinct is proactively to control framing; Susan to advise.

## My takeaway
We are in a fundamentally defensible position. The accounting was clean, the audit was clean, and the underlying transactions reflect normal channel operations. The risk is presentation, not substance. Susan's team should drive how this is sequenced and framed.
